title: Non-equilibrium quantum thermodynamics of a memory-bearing open-system process
authors: Biagio G. Banigi, Eric Lutz, Mauro Paternostro
abstract: We show the emergence of memory effects in the dynamics of a driven two-level system interacting with a composite environment, and analyze their influence on work, heat and entropy production. We further investigate how the interplay between driving, dissipation and memory effects, stemming from the finiteness of the environment, shapes the thermodynamic response of the system, thus providing insight into quantum thermodynamics beyond the Markovian approximation.
summary: The paper analyzes non-equilibrium thermodynamics for a qubit coupled to a structured environment. It demonstrates that memory effects, stemming from the finite nature of the bath (harmonic oscillator), cause deviations from Markovian predictions. These deviations are quantified by analyzing the time evolution and potential sign changes in the entropy production rate.
main_problem: The study investigates non-equilibrium quantum thermodynamics for open systems, specifically analyzing how memory effects influence work, heat, and entropy production beyond the standard Markovian approximation.
main_result: Non-Markovian dynamics, evidenced by backflow of information (e.g., negative entropy production rate $\sigma\Sigma(t)$), significantly shape the system's thermodynamic response compared to Markovian limits.
method: The analysis employs quantum master equations and diagnostic tools like the BLP measure and the time evolution of entropy production ($\sigma\Sigma(t)$) to quantify non-Markovianity.
model_or_system: The model is a two-level system (qubit) coupled to a composite environment consisting of a harmonic oscillator and a thermal bath, driven by an external field. The dynamics are governed by a Lindblad-like master equation.
key_observables: Work rate ($\dot{W}$), heat rate ($\dot{Q}$), entropy production rate ($\sigma\Sigma(t)$), and the trace distance $D(t)$.
important_parameters: Coupling strengths ($J$, $\Gamma$), driving frequency ($\omega_D$), and the regime comparison between Markovian ($J=0$) and non-Markovian ($J>0$) dynamics.
main_assumptions: The weak-coupling regime is assumed, and the analysis often relies on deriving effective equations of motion by tracing out environmental degrees of freedom.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate time evolution plots showing population decay, coherence damping, and crucially, the non-monotonic or negative behavior of $\sigma\Sigma(t)$ when memory effects are present.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theoretical framework by defining thermodynamic rates (Work/Heat) and then analyzes the dynamics using both quantum information measures (BLP distance) and thermodynamic diagnostics ($\sigma\Sigma(t)$), comparing results across different coupling strengths to isolate the role of memory.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it tackles the fundamental limitations of standard Markovian descriptions in open quantum systems, providing quantitative tools ($\sigma\Sigma(t)$) to detect and characterize non-Markovian energy flow.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Sketch of the open system process described in Sec. II. A two-level system is coupled to a harmonic oscillator (with strength J) and a thermal bath (Γ), and it is driven by an external field (described by the parameter λ).

FIG. 2. Time evolution of the (a) trace distance D(ρ1, ρ2) and (b) the trace distance rate of change σ(t, ρ1,2(0)) as defined, respectively, in (7) and (10). The simulations make use of states ρ1(0) = |+⟩⟨+| and ρ2(0) = |−⟩⟨−| and physical parameters ω = 1, J = 0.2, p = 5, Γ = 10−3, n = 3, ωD = 1/3, λ = 3 × 10−3, ωt f = 50π. All parameters are expressed in dimensionless units by fixing the system frequency ω as the reference scale.

FIG. 3. Temporal behavior of the (a) populations and (b) coherences of the system’s density matrix plotted against the dimensionless time ωt. We only report the real part of coherences (Re[ρeg] = Re[ρge]), since the imaginary one is quantitatively negligible. In all the simula- tions, if not otherwise specified, we used the same parameters as in Fig. 2.

FIG. 4. Panel (a): Temporal behavior of work done on the system, plotted against the dimensionless time ωt. Panel (b): We show the dynamics of the heat Q(t) exchanged between the qubit and the environment for the same situation displayed in panel (a), along with the system populations for comparison. In all the simulations, if not otherwise specified, we used the same parameters as in Fig. 2.

FIG. 5. Time evolution of the entropy production rate σΣ(t) for three different dynamical configurations. In panel (a) we set Γ = λ = 0; in panel (b) Γ = 10−3, λ = 0; and in panel (c) Γ = 10−3, λ = 3 × 10−3. In all cases, ω = 1, J = 0.2, p = 5, n = 3, ωD = 1/3, ωt f = 50π. All parameters are expressed in dimensionless units by fixing the system frequency ω as the reference scale.
